tagline: Calculate your boxing reach advantage, ape index, and optimal fighting distance
keywords: boxing reach, reach advantage, ape index, wingspan, fighting distance

faq.question: What is the ape index?
faq.answer: Ape index is your wingspan minus your height. A positive ape index means your arms are longer than you are tall — an advantage in boxing, climbing, and swimming. The average is 0 (wingspan equals height).

faq.question: How important is reach in boxing?
faq.answer: Reach is one of the most significant physical advantages. Fighters with longer reach can hit opponents while staying out of range. However, shorter-armed fighters can overcome this with head movement and inside fighting.

faq.question: What is the average boxing reach?
faq.answer: Average reach is approximately equal to height. Professional boxers average about 71-73 inches at middleweight. Notable long reaches include Tyson Fury (85") and Thomas Hearns (78").

formulas: Reach = Wingspan ÷ 2 (arm length from center chest)
Ape Index = Wingspan - Height
Reach Advantage = Your Wingspan - Opponent Wingspan
Positive ape index = longer arms relative to height

tagline: Calculate cycling power output, watts per kilo, and climbing speed
keywords: cycling power, watts per kg, FTP calculator, cycling watts, climbing speed

faq.question: What is a good watts per kg for cycling?
faq.answer: Beginner: 1.5-2.5 W/kg. Recreational: 2.5-3.2. Club racer: 3.2-4.0. Competitive amateur: 4.0-5.0. Professional: 5.5-7.0 W/kg. World Tour winners sustain 6.0-6.5 W/kg on major climbs.

faq.question: What is FTP in cycling?
faq.answer: Functional Threshold Power (FTP) is the highest average power you can sustain for approximately one hour. It's the gold standard for measuring cycling fitness and setting training zones.

faq.question: How does weight affect climbing speed?
faq.answer: On steep climbs, watts per kilogram determines speed. Losing 1 kg at the same power is equivalent to gaining about 3-5 watts on a 7% climb. This is why climbers tend to be lighter.

formulas: Watts per Kg = Power ÷ (Rider Weight in kg)
Climbing Speed ≈ Power ÷ (Weight × g × Gradient + Rolling Resistance)
Rolling Resistance ≈ Weight × g × 0.005
FTP zones: Endurance 55-75%, Tempo 75-90%, Threshold 90-105%

tagline: Estimate lacrosse shot speed based on stick speed, technique, and ball energy
keywords: lacrosse shot speed, shooting velocity, lacrosse mph, stick speed

faq.question: How fast do lacrosse players shoot?
faq.answer: Youth: 40-60 mph. High school: 60-80 mph. College: 80-100 mph. Professional MLL/PLL: 95-115 mph. The fastest recorded lacrosse shot is around 116 mph.

faq.question: Does stick length affect shot speed?
faq.answer: Longer sticks provide more leverage for faster shots but are harder to control. Attack sticks (40") offer quick release, while long poles (60") can generate more power with proper technique.

faq.question: How can I increase my lacrosse shot speed?
faq.answer: Focus on: hip and core rotation (the primary power source), wrist snap at release, proper weight transfer from back to front foot, and using the full range of the stick's flex.

formulas: Shot Speed ≈ Base × Arm Speed% × Technique Factor × Lever Factor × Strength Factor
Kinetic Energy = ½ × Ball Mass × Velocity²
Goalie Reaction Time = Distance ÷ Ball Velocity
Ball mass = 142g (5 oz), standard lacrosse ball

tagline: Estimate crop yield per acre and total harvest for common field crops
keywords: crop yield, yield per acre, harvest calculator, farm production, bushels per acre

faq.question: What is the average corn yield per acre?
faq.answer: The US national average corn yield is about 175 bushels per acre (2023). Top farmers in the Corn Belt can achieve 250-300+ bushels. Yield depends heavily on soil quality, weather, hybrid selection, and management.

faq.question: How much is a bushel of corn worth?
faq.answer: Corn prices fluctuate between $3.50-$7.00 per bushel depending on market conditions. A bushel of corn weighs 56 lbs. At $4.50/bushel and 175 bu/acre, gross revenue is about $788/acre.

faq.question: What factors affect crop yield?
faq.answer: Key factors: soil fertility, rainfall/irrigation, seed genetics, planting date and density, pest and disease pressure, weed management, and weather conditions (especially during pollination for corn).

formulas: Yield per Acre = Average Yield × (Yield Factor%)
Total Yield = Yield per Acre × Total Acres
Gross Revenue = Total Yield × Market Price
Revenue per Acre = Gross Revenue ÷ Acres

tagline: Calculate daily feed requirements and costs for cattle, hogs, poultry, and other livestock
keywords: livestock feed, cattle feed, animal feed cost, feed calculator, hay calculator

faq.question: How much does a beef cow eat per day?
faq.answer: A 1,200 lb beef cow eats about 24-30 lbs of dry matter daily (2-2.5% of body weight). This could be 30-35 lbs of hay or a mix of hay and grain. Lactating cows eat more.

faq.question: How much hay does a cow need per year?
faq.answer: A beef cow needs about 4-5 tons of hay per year (roughly 25-30 lbs/day × 365 days). If supplementing with grain or pasture, hay needs decrease proportionally.

faq.question: What is the feed conversion ratio?
faq.answer: Feed conversion ratio (FCR) is pounds of feed per pound of gain. Beef cattle: 6-8:1. Hogs: 3-4:1. Chickens (broiler): 1.8-2:1. Lower is more efficient. Poultry is the most feed-efficient livestock.

formulas: Daily Feed = Animal Daily Requirement × Head Count
Total Feed = Daily Feed × Feeding Period (days)
Total Cost = (Total Feed ÷ 2000) × Cost per Ton
Feed as % of body weight: 2-3% for cattle, 3-5% for hogs

tagline: Calculate net farm profit per acre including all input costs and revenue
keywords: farm profit, farming income, cost per acre, agricultural profit, farm budget

faq.question: What is the average profit per acre for farming?
faq.answer: Net profit varies widely: $50-$200/acre for corn in good years, but losses are common in bad years. The 2023 USDA average for corn was about $120/acre net return. Specialty crops can earn $500-$2,000+/acre.

faq.question: What are the biggest farming expenses?
faq.answer: Typical breakdown: Land costs (25-35%), fertilizer (15-20%), machinery/fuel (10-15%), seed (10-15%), chemicals (5-8%), crop insurance (3-5%), and labor (5-10% for row crops).

faq.question: How do farmers make a profit with thin margins?
faq.answer: Scale is key — 500+ acres spreads fixed costs. Also: forward contracting to lock in prices, crop insurance, government programs, diversification, precision agriculture to optimize inputs, and value-added processing.

formulas: Total Revenue = Revenue per Acre × Acres
Total Costs = (Seed + Fertilizer + Chemicals + Machinery + Land + Insurance) × Acres
Net Profit = Total Revenue - Total Costs
Breakeven Yield = Costs per Acre ÷ Price per Bushel

tagline: Calculate bullet muzzle energy, momentum, and power factor from velocity and weight
keywords: muzzle energy, bullet energy, foot-pounds, power factor, ballistics

faq.question: How do you calculate muzzle energy?
faq.answer: Muzzle energy (ft-lbs) = (Bullet Weight in grains × Velocity² in fps) ÷ 450,240. For example, a 124gr 9mm at 1,150 fps produces about 364 ft-lbs of energy.

faq.question: What is a good muzzle energy for self-defense?
faq.answer: Most experts recommend a minimum of 250-300 ft-lbs for self-defense. Standard 9mm (124gr at 1,150 fps) produces ~364 ft-lbs. .45 ACP (230gr at 830 fps) produces ~352 ft-lbs.

faq.question: What is Power Factor?
faq.answer: Power Factor = (Bullet Weight × Velocity) ÷ 1,000. In USPSA competition: Major requires 165+ PF, Minor requires 125+ PF. Higher PF means larger scoring zones count in competition.

formulas: Energy (ft-lbs) = (Weight (gr) × Velocity²) ÷ 450,240
Momentum = (Weight ÷ 7000) × Velocity
Power Factor = (Weight × Velocity) ÷ 1,000
Taylor KO = (Weight × Velocity × Diameter) ÷ 7,000

tagline: Calculate shotgun pattern spread, pellet count, and density at various distances
keywords: shotgun pattern, spread calculator, choke pattern, pellet density, shot size

faq.question: What choke should I use for trap/skeet?
faq.answer: Skeet: Skeet or Improved Cylinder for close shots. Trap: Modified to Full for longer shots. Sporting clays: start with Improved Cylinder/Modified and adjust based on station distances.

faq.question: How far does a shotgun pattern spread?
faq.answer: With a Modified choke, expect roughly 30" spread at 30 yards, with about 65% of pellets in a 30" circle. Tighter chokes concentrate the pattern; open chokes spread it faster.

faq.question: What is pattern percentage?
faq.answer: Pattern percentage is the fraction of pellets landing within a 30-inch circle at 40 yards. Full choke: 70-80%. Modified: 55-65%. Improved Cylinder: 45-55%. Cylinder: 35-45%.

formulas: Pattern Diameter ≈ Base + Distance × (1 - Choke%) × Spread Factor
Total Pellets = Pellets per Oz × Shot Charge (oz)
Pellets in Circle ≈ Total × Choke% × (Circle Area ÷ Pattern Area)
Density = Pellets in Circle ÷ Circle Area (sq inches)

tagline: Calculate firearm recoil energy, velocity, and impulse from load data
keywords: recoil calculator, recoil energy, free recoil, felt recoil, gun recoil

faq.question: How is recoil calculated?
faq.answer: Free recoil uses Newton's third law: the gun's momentum equals the bullet + gas momentum. Recoil energy = (Gun Weight × Recoil Velocity²) ÷ (2 × 32.174). Heavier guns absorb more recoil.

faq.question: What is a comfortable recoil level?
faq.answer: Under 10 ft-lbs: very comfortable (9mm pistol, .223). 10-20 ft-lbs: moderate (.308, 12ga field load). 20-30 ft-lbs: stout (12ga magnum, .300 Win Mag). Over 40 ft-lbs: punishing (.375 H&H, .458).

faq.question: How can I reduce recoil?
faq.answer: Heavier gun (most effective), muzzle brake (30-50% reduction), recoil pad, gas-operated action (vs bolt), lighter bullets at same energy, or smaller powder charge.

formulas: Recoil Velocity = (Bullet Mass × MV + Powder Mass × Gas Velocity) ÷ Gun Mass
Gas Velocity ≈ Muzzle Velocity × 1.75
Recoil Energy = (Gun Weight × Recoil Velocity²) ÷ (2 × 32.174)
Recoil Impulse = Gun Weight × Recoil Velocity ÷ 32.174

tagline: Calculate wind turbine energy output and payback period based on wind speed and turbine size
keywords: wind turbine output, wind energy calculator, wind power, small wind turbine

faq.question: How much power does a small wind turbine produce?
faq.answer: A small residential turbine (10-15 ft rotor) in 12 mph average winds produces 2,000-5,000 kWh/year — about 20-40% of average US home usage. Output scales with the cube of wind speed.

faq.question: What wind speed do I need for a wind turbine?
faq.answer: Minimum average of 9-10 mph for viability. 12+ mph average is ideal. Wind resources vary greatly even within small areas — get a proper wind assessment before investing.

faq.question: Is a home wind turbine worth it?
faq.answer: Depends on wind resource, electricity costs, and incentives. In good wind areas (12+ mph avg) with high electricity rates ($0.15+/kWh), payback can be 10-15 years. Tax credits can cut this significantly.

formulas: Power = ½ × Air Density × Swept Area × Wind Speed³ × Efficiency
Swept Area = π × (Rotor Diameter / 2)²
Annual Output = Rated Power × 8,760 hours × Capacity Factor
Capacity Factor ≈ 0.20-0.35 for small turbines

tagline: Calculate savings from installing a geothermal heat pump vs traditional HVAC
keywords: geothermal savings, ground source heat pump, geothermal cost, GSHP calculator

faq.question: How much does geothermal save on heating and cooling?
faq.answer: Geothermal heat pumps typically reduce heating and cooling costs by 50-70%. With a COP of 4.0, you get 4 units of heating for every 1 unit of electricity — 3-4× more efficient than conventional systems.

faq.question: How much does a geothermal system cost?
faq.answer: Residential geothermal: $15,000-$35,000 installed, depending on home size and loop type. The 30% federal tax credit (through 2032) brings this down significantly. Systems last 25-50 years.

faq.question: What is COP (Coefficient of Performance)?
faq.answer: COP measures heat pump efficiency: heat output ÷ electrical input. A COP of 4.0 means 400% efficiency — 4 units of heat per 1 unit of electricity. Geothermal COP ranges from 3.5-5.0.

formulas: Net Cost = System Cost - (System Cost × Tax Credit%)
Geothermal Bill = Current Bill ÷ COP
Annual Savings = Current Bill - Geothermal Bill
Payback = Net Cost ÷ Annual Savings

tagline: Size your off-grid battery bank for reliable energy storage and backup power
keywords: off grid battery, battery bank sizing, solar battery, energy storage, battery backup

faq.question: How many batteries do I need for off-grid?
faq.answer: For a home using 30 kWh/day with 3 days autonomy at 80% DOD: you need about 112.5 kWh of battery capacity. In 48V LiFePO4, that's about 2,344 Ah — roughly 5-8 large battery modules.

faq.question: LiFePO4 vs lead-acid for off-grid?
faq.answer: LiFePO4 costs 2× more upfront but lasts 3-5× longer, has 80% DOD (vs 50% for lead-acid), weighs 1/3 as much, and needs no maintenance. Lifetime cost is usually lower with lithium.

faq.question: What is depth of discharge (DOD)?
faq.answer: DOD is how much of the battery's capacity you use. LiFePO4: 80-90% DOD safely. AGM: 50% max recommended. Flooded lead-acid: 50% max. Deeper discharge shortens battery life.

formulas: Total Capacity = (Daily Usage × Autonomy Days) ÷ Depth of Discharge
Amp-Hours = Total Capacity (Wh) ÷ System Voltage
Battery Cost = Capacity (kWh) × Cost per kWh
LiFePO4: 5000+ cycles at 80% DOD; Lead-acid: 1000 at 50% DOD
